title: Common Loop Design Pitfalls
concept: State loss, loose parser patterns, lack of escape hatches, and missing cost ceilings.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: how can an agent tell it's stuck?

options.text: It notices zero real progress across several turns and self-corrects
It waits for a human to unplug it

exercises.explain: A good agent watches its own progress. If nothing useful changes for a few turns, that's a signal to try a new approach instead of looping forever.

cards.title: Where loops go wrong
cards.body: Agent loops are powerful, but small design mistakes can make them forget, misread, or run forever. Let's spot the four most common traps.

cards.title: Four classic pitfalls
cards.body: State loss: the agent forgets earlier facts. Loose parser: it misreads its own output. No escape hatch: it can't quit a bad path. No cost ceiling: it runs with no spending or time limit.

cards.title: A road trip gone wrong
cards.body: Forgetting your destination (state loss), misreading signs (loose parser), no way to turn around (no escape hatch), and no gas budget (no cost ceiling) all ruin the trip in different ways.

cards.title: The endless loop
cards.body: An agent with no cost ceiling keeps calling tools trying to book a flight it can't find. It burns hundreds of API calls before anyone notices. A simple limit would have stopped it.

cards.title: Myth: smart agents don't need limits
cards.body: False! Even the best agent can get confused or stuck. Escape hatches and cost ceilings are safety nets that protect against surprises, not signs of a weak agent.

cards.title: Fragile vs sturdy loop
cards.body: One design fails silently; the other fails safely.

left.text: Forgets facts, misreads output, no stop button, runs forever

right.text: Keeps memory, parses strictly, can quit, has a hard budget

exercises.prompt: An agent keeps re-asking for the user's name it was already told. Which pitfall is this?
exercises.explain: Re-asking for info it already had means the agent is forgetting earlier facts. That's state loss: the loop isn't carrying important memory forward between turns.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A cost ceiling limits how many calls or how much time an agent can use before it must stop.
exercises.explain: Correct! A cost ceiling is a hard cap on resources, so a confused agent can't run forever and rack up huge bills.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about safe design.
exercises.sentence: An escape hatch lets an agent quit a bad path instead of looping forever.
exercises.explain: An escape hatch is a built-in exit. Without it, an agent stuck on a bad plan can never break free on its own.

exercises.prompt: Which explanation of 'loose parser pattern' was written by an AI?

options.text: A loose parser reads the agent's output too flexibly, so it can misread the wrong text as a valid action or tool call.
loose parser thing when code no good and break sometimes idk
The parser is a snack you eat while the loop runs and it tastes loose.

exercises.explain: The first is clear, accurate, and complete, matching AI-style explanation. The others are broken or nonsense, so the AI one is option A.

exercises.prompt: An agent forgot its goal, misread its own JSON output as a valid command, and ran 500 tool calls overnight with no one stopping it. Which single fix addresses the most pitfalls at once?

options.text: Add a hard cost ceiling AND strict parsing AND persistent state, since each pitfall needs its own targeted fix
Remove all memory so nothing can be forgotten
Let it run longer so it fixes itself

exercises.explain: There's no magic single fix: state loss needs persistent memory, loose parsing needs strict output rules, and runaway loops need a cost ceiling plus an escape hatch. Sturdy agents combine all these guardrails. You've now got the core toolkit for designing reliable agent loops!
